---
title: "Two Weeks in Quarto / Pandoc / LaTeX Hell"
subtitle: "Tracking down `Extra }, or forgotten \\endgroup` across three tool layers"
author: "Michael Friendly (with Claude assistance)"
date: "2026-04-13"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    number_sections: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# Background

I am writing a book, *Visualizing Multivariate Data and Models in R*, using
[Quarto](https://quarto.org/) with both HTML and PDF (via LaTeX) output.
The PDF uses the `krantz` document class (CRC Press house style), compiled
with XeLaTeX. The build pipeline is roughly:

```
.qmd source files
  → knitr (R code execution)
  → pandoc (markdown → LaTeX, with citeproc for citations)
  → XeLaTeX (LaTeX → PDF)
  → makeindex (subject index)
  → authorindex Perl script (author index)
  → XeLaTeX (final PDF)
```

I have several helper R functions in `R/common.R` that generate inline LaTeX
for formatted, indexed references to R packages, datasets, and functions:

- `pkg("ggplot2")` → `\texttt{\textbf{ggplot2}}` + index entries
- `dataset("Prestige")` → `\texttt{Prestige}` + index entries
- `func("lm()")` → `\texttt{lm()}` + index entries

These are called as inline R code in the `.qmd` source:

```markdown
The function `r func("lm()")` fits linear models...
```

**Everything was working perfectly** — both HTML and PDF builds succeeded,
the subject index and author index were generated correctly — until I tried
to fix a long-standing annoyance with function names containing underscores.

---

# The Triggering Change: Fixing `_` in Function Names

Function names like `stat_ellipse()`, `geom_smooth()`, and `facet_wrap()`
contain underscores. In LaTeX math mode, `_` is the subscript character
(catcode 8). In text mode it can cause "Missing \$" errors if it appears
unescaped in index entries.

The fix was conceptually simple: add an `escape()` helper that converts `_`
to `\_`, and use two-argument `\ixfunc{sort-key}{display-name}` so that the
sort key could use the raw `_` (fine for makeindex) while the display name
used `\_` (required for LaTeX typesetting in the `.ind` file).

In `R/common.R`, `func()` went from:

```r
ref <- paste0(ref, "\n\\ixfunc{", fname, "}{", escape(fname), "}\n")
```

to (after further fixes):

```r
ref <- paste0(ref, "\\ixfunc{", fname, "}{", escape(fname), "}")
```

This was committed around April 4. The PDF build broke immediately —
not with a "Missing \$" error from `_`, but with something completely
different and far more baffling.

---

# The Error

```
! Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup.
l.4192 ...citeproc{ref-Blishen-etal-1987}{1987}).}
```

This error appeared on every PDF build attempt. The offending line (in
Quarto's internal `index.tex`, which is what xelatex actually compiles)
was the closing brace of a footnote in Chapter 4:

```latex
\texttt{Prestige}%
  \index{Prestige@\texttt{Prestige} data}%
  \index{datasets!Prestige@\texttt{Prestige}}%.\footnote{The dataset was
  collected by Bernard Blishen, William Carroll and Catherine Moore, but
  apparently unpublished. A version updated to the 1981 census is
  described in Blishen et al. (\citeproc{ref-Blishen-etal-1987}{1987}).}
```

The `.qmd` source at this location was entirely innocent:

```markdown
recorded in `r dataset("Prestige")`.[^prestige-src]

[^prestige-src]: The dataset was collected by Bernard Blishen...
  described in @Blishen-etal-1987.
```

---

# Investigation: Week One

## First Diagnosis: Multi-line Inline R Output

The pattern `%.\footnote{` jumped out immediately. In LaTeX, `%` is a comment
character that silences everything to the end of the line — including the period
and the `\footnote{` that opens the footnote. LaTeX then encounters the
footnote text as orphaned content, and the closing `}` of the footnote is
"extra" with nothing to close.

**How did `%` get there?** Pandoc adds `%` at line breaks when it formats
multi-line raw inline LaTeX spans to prevent spurious spaces. If an inline R
expression returns a string containing `\n`, knitr stores it as a raw LaTeX
inline spanning multiple lines. When pandoc writes the `.tex` file, it inserts
`%` at each break.

So the culprit was `dataset("Prestige")` returning:

```
\texttt{Prestige}\n\index{Prestige@\texttt{Prestige} data}\n\index{datasets!...}
```

The `\n` characters caused pandoc to format this as:

```latex
\texttt{Prestige}%
  \index{Prestige@\texttt{Prestige} data}%
  \index{datasets!Prestige@\texttt{Prestige}}%
```

And then `%` at the end of the last `\index{}%` line commented out the
`.\footnote{...}` that followed on the same line.

**Fix #1:** Remove `\n` from `dataset()` in `common.R`. The old code was:

```r
# old — causes multi-line output → pandoc adds %
ref <- paste0(ref, "\n\\index{...", dname, "...}\n\\index{...}\n")
```

The new code used a macro call instead:

```r
# new — single-line, pandoc passes it through unchanged
ref <- paste0(ref, "\\ixd{", dname, "}")
```

**Result:** Error persisted. The `%` was gone, but so was something else.

## Second Diagnosis: Stale Freeze Cache

Quarto's freeze mechanism stores R execution results in
`.quarto/_freeze/<chapter>/execute-results/tex.json`. When the source
hasn't changed, Quarto reuses cached results instead of re-running R.

Inspection of the freeze cache with `grep` showed:

```
# In tex.json (freeze cache, regenerated April 12):
recorded in \\texttt{Prestige}\\ixd{Prestige}.[^prestige-src]
```

But in `Vis-MLM.tex` (the `keep-tex: true` copy of the last *successful* build,
timestamped April 10):

```latex
\texttt{Prestige} %
        \index{data sets!Prestige}.\footnote{The dataset was collected
```

The freeze cache had the **new** `\ixd{Prestige}` format. The `.tex` copy had
an **old** `\index{data sets!Prestige}` format — a completely different format
that predated the `\ixd{}` macro. Something was happening *between* the freeze
cache being read and the `.tex` file being written.

**Important clarification about Quarto's tex files:** Quarto's internal build
compiles `index.tex` (named after the entry point `index.qmd`). The file
`Vis-MLM.tex` is a separate copy, saved by `keep-tex: true` and named after
`output-file: Vis-MLM` in `_quarto.yml`. It is only written when a build
progresses far enough — on a failed build, it may be stale from a previous
run. This caused endless confusion: `Vis-MLM.tex` was from April 10 and
compiled fine in TeXStudio (it predated the `\ixd{}` expansion); `index.tex`
(the actual compiled file) had the current, broken content.

**Hypothesis:** The freeze cache was stale and being read from a previous
build where `dataset()` still produced the old multi-line format.

**Fix #2:** Delete all 35 `tex.json` freeze cache files. Force full
re-execution of every chapter.

**Result:** Error persisted. The same discrepancy remained.

## Third Diagnosis: parse-latex Filter

The project uses the `tarleb/parse-latex` Quarto extension, which expands
user-defined LaTeX macros for HTML output. The extension was listed in the
global `filters:` section of `_quarto.yml` (not just under `format: html:`).

If `parse-latex` was running during the PDF build as well as the HTML build,
it might be expanding `\ixd{Prestige}` — reading the `\providecommand{\ixd}`
definition from the document content and substituting the full multi-line
`\index{}` expansion. Pandoc would then add `%` at the line breaks, re-creating
the `%.\footnote{}` problem.

**Fix #3:** Move `parse-latex` from global filters to `format: html: filters:`
only.

The `parse-latex` Lua filter had an explicit guard at the top:

```lua
-- Return an empty filter if the target format is LaTeX
if FORMAT:match 'latex' then
  return {}
end
```

The assumption was: for PDF builds, Quarto's pandoc pass uses `FORMAT = "latex"`,
so this guard would fire and the filter would return empty. **This assumption
was wrong.**

Quarto's PDF build pipeline passes a FORMAT value that does **not** contain
the string `"latex"` (it may be `"pdf"` or a Quarto-internal format identifier).
So `FORMAT:match 'latex'` evaluated to `nil` (falsy), the guard did NOT fire,
and parse-latex ran for the PDF build — expanding `\ixd{Prestige}` into its
multi-line `\index{}` definition, which pandoc then wrapped in `%` comments.

The fix was to invert the guard:

```lua
-- Only run for HTML output
if not FORMAT:match 'html' then
  return {}
end
```

This correctly skips the filter for any non-HTML format (PDF, docx, etc.).

**Result:** This fix eliminates the `%.\footnote{}` mechanism. Combined with
the `preamble.tex` fix below, the build succeeds.

## Red Herrings Along the Way

- **Quarto version:** The error started after upgrading Quarto from 1.8.x to
  1.9.36. Reverting to 1.8.25 was tried as a diagnostic — to determine whether
  the Quarto upgrade itself was responsible. Error persisted on 1.8.25, ruling
  out Quarto as the cause. (Plan: upgrade back to latest once the build is
  confirmed working.)

- **`sec-labels: arabic`:** This invalid option had been added to the `crossref:`
  block in `_quarto.yml` (attempting to fix a separate HTML cross-reference
  issue). It was removed, but didn't affect the PDF error.

- **The timestamp mystery:** The `.quarto/_freeze/04-multivariate_plots/execute-results/tex.json`
  had timestamp 14:58 (April 12) while `Vis-MLM.tex` showed 18:48 (April 10).
  This suggested the `.tex` file was from an *older* build and never got
  regenerated during the April 12 build — possibly because Quarto's PDF build
  failed before the pandoc step, or due to Windows NTFS timestamp quirks. This
  led to considerable confusion about whether fixes had actually taken effect.

---

# The Actual Root Cause (Week Two)

## Following the Chain

The real clue was in the `\citeproc` macro. Pandoc generates this for every
citation; it appears in `Vis-MLM.tex` as:

```latex
% In pandoc-generated preamble (lines 141–154 of Vis-MLM.tex):
\NewDocumentCommand\citeproc{mm}{%
  \begingroup\def\citeproctext{#2}\cite{#1}\endgroup}
```

Translation: every `[@Blishen-etal-1987]` becomes
`\citeproc{ref-Blishen-etal-1987}{1987}`, which expands to
`\begingroup\cite{ref-Blishen-etal-1987}\endgroup`.

Now look at `latex/preamble.tex`. To make the author index work with
pandoc citeproc (which doesn't emit plain `\cite{}` calls), I had added a
patch to `\@citex` (LaTeX's internal citation dispatcher) inside
`\AtBeginDocument{}`:

```latex
\AtBeginDocument{%
  \let\@ai@orig@citex\@citex
  \def\@citex[#1]#2{\@ai@orig@citex[#1]{#2}\@aicitey{#2}}%
  ...
}
```

The `\@aicitey{#2}` call writes citation-page data to the `.ain` file
for the authorindex Perl script.

**The fatal combination:**

1. `\citeproc{key}{text}` inside `\footnote{}` expands to `\begingroup\cite{key}\endgroup`
2. `\cite{key}` triggers the patched `\@citex`
3. Patched `\@citex` calls `\@aicitey{key}`, which does `\write` to the aux file
4. `\write` inside a `\footnote{}` context on the first LaTeX pass — where
   footnote content is "harvested" into a token list for deferred typesetting —
   causes the group boundaries from `\begingroup...\endgroup` to become
   misaligned
5. LaTeX reports `Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup` when it encounters the
   closing `}` of the footnote

This is a variant of the classic **fragile command in moving argument** problem.
`\footnote{}` moves its argument (the footnote text is stored as a token list
and typeset later on a different page). Commands that open and close groups,
or that do `\write` operations, can behave differently when "moved" than when
typeset in place.

## Why It Started With the `func()` Fix

The `func()` fix didn't directly cause the `\@citex` patch to misbehave — it
had always been misbehaving. What changed was that the April 4 work involved
multiple round-trips of building and debugging. At some point:

- The `\@citex` patch was written to intercept citations for the author index
- A Quarto upgrade (1.9.36) likely changed how pandoc's preamble was generated
- The particular `.tex` file that landed on disk combined the active `\@citex`
  patch with a `\footnote{}` containing `\citeproc{...}` such that the error
  became reproducible every build

The footnote in ch04 about the Prestige dataset source
(`[^prestige-src]` → `@Blishen-etal-1987`) was the first — and only — citation
inside a footnote in that chapter. Without that citation, the error wouldn't
trigger there. With it, every build failed at exactly that footnote.

## Why `dataset()` Multi-line Output Was a Separate Concurrent Problem

While the `\@citex` patch was the root cause of the `Extra }`, the
multi-line `\n` output from `dataset()`, `pkg()`, and `package()` was a
*concurrent* problem that would have caused the *same error* by a different
mechanism: `%.\footnote{...}` commenting out the footnote opening. Both
problems needed fixing:

1. `dataset()`, `pkg()`, `package()` — remove `\n` from around `\\ixp{}`,
   `\\ixd{}` etc.
2. `preamble.tex` — disable the `\@citex` patch that caused
   `\citeproc`-in-footnote to fail

---

# The Fix

## `latex/preamble.tex`

Disable the `\@citex` patch (it was only needed if authorindex needed to
re-run dynamically; since `index.ain` is pre-built, it's unnecessary):

```latex
\AtBeginDocument{%
  % Disabled: \@citex patch causes "Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup" when
  % \citeproc (used by pandoc) is inside a \footnote{} on the first LaTeX pass.
  % index.ain is pre-built; authorindex does not need to re-run.
  %  \let\@ai@orig@citex\@citex
  %  \def\@citex[#1]#2{\@ai@orig@citex[#1]{#2}\@aicitey{#2}}%
  \if@filesw\immediate\write\@auxout{...}\fi
  \includepdf[pages=1]{images/cover/cover-peng-scaled.pdf}
}
```

Add an explicit override for `\citeproc` as defense-in-depth (pandoc's own
template also adds this in recent versions, but belt-and-suspenders):

```latex
% Pandoc's \citeproc{key}{text} uses \begingroup\cite{key}\endgroup.
% On the first LaTeX pass (or when .aux is stale from a failed build),
% undefined citations inside \footnote{} cause "Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup".
% \renewcommand does not reliably override \NewDocumentCommand-defined commands
% in modern LaTeX (xparse internal dispatch). Use \def at the TeX primitive level.
\def\citeproc#1#2{#2}
```

## `_extensions/tarleb/parse-latex/parse-latex.lua`

Change the format guard from matching `'latex'` (which Quarto's PDF pandoc
pass does NOT use) to excluding non-HTML formats:

```lua
-- WRONG: Quarto's PDF pandoc pass may not use FORMAT = "latex"
if FORMAT:match 'latex' then
  return {}
end

-- RIGHT: only run for HTML
if not FORMAT:match 'html' then
  return {}
end
```

## `R/common.R`

Remove all `\n` characters from around LaTeX macro calls in the index-entry
generating functions. The principle: **inline R code output must be
single-line**, because pandoc wraps multi-line raw inline LaTeX in `%` comments.

```r
# WRONG — multi-line output; pandoc adds % at breaks
ref <- paste0(ref, "\n\\ixp{", package, "}\n")

# RIGHT — single-line; pandoc passes through unchanged
ref <- paste0(ref, "\\ixp{", package, "}")
```

This applies to all four functions: `pkg()`, `package()`, `dataset()`, `func()`.

---

# Lessons Learned

## Quarto/Pandoc ≠ LaTeX

When writing in Quarto for PDF output, you are operating in **three separate
layers**:

1. **R layer** (knitr): generates strings that become raw LaTeX
2. **Pandoc layer**: converts Markdown + raw LaTeX snippets into a `.tex` file,
   applying filters and adding `%` at multi-line raw inline breaks
3. **LaTeX layer** (XeLaTeX): compiles the `.tex` file, expanding macros,
   processing groups, handling fragile/moving arguments

A bug can live at any layer, but its *symptoms* appear at a different layer.
The `Extra }` error was a LaTeX-layer symptom of a pandoc-layer mechanism
(`%` at line breaks) caused by an R-layer problem (`\n` in string output) —
AND simultaneously a LaTeX-layer problem (fragile `\@citex` patch inside
moving argument) also manifesting at the same location.

## The Freeze Cache Complicates Debugging

Quarto's freeze cache stores knitr execution results in `.quarto/_freeze/`.
When debugging "why does my `.tex` file have wrong content", you are looking at
a file generated from the cached results, not the current R code. Changes to
`common.R` won't appear in the `.tex` file until the cache is invalidated.
Quarto invalidates the cache when the `.qmd` source changes, but NOT when a
sourced helper file like `common.R` changes (in all configurations). This led
to much confusion about whether fixes had taken effect.

**Defensive practice:** After changing `common.R`, delete or force-invalidate
the relevant freeze cache entries before rebuilding.

## `\write` in Footnotes Is a Classic LaTeX Trap

If you are using any LaTeX package or macro that writes to auxiliary files
from within citations (`\cite`, `\bibitem`, etc.), and those citations can
appear inside `\footnote{}`, you are living dangerously. The `\footnote{}`
environment moves its argument, and commands that manipulate group level or
do file I/O can misbehave inside moving arguments.

The `authorindex` package patches `\@citex` to intercept citation calls and
record author-page data. This is fine for normal in-text citations. It is NOT
fine when `\citeproc{key}{text}` calls `\cite{key}` inside a footnote,
because the `\begingroup...\endgroup` wrapper around `\cite` inside
`\footnote{}` hits the `\write` in the patched `\@citex` at the wrong time.

**The fix:** Either use `\protect\cite` inside footnotes (the traditional
LaTeX answer), or — for pandoc citeproc documents where bibliography is
already pre-formatted — bypass `\cite` entirely with `\def\citeproc#1#2{#2}`.

## Quarto PDF Builds Are Not Reproducible Without Attention

The Quarto build is not a single atomic step. The knitr step, the pandoc step,
and the XeLaTeX step each produce intermediate files. If any step fails:

- The freeze cache (`.quarto/_freeze/*/tex.json`) may have been updated
- `Vis-MLM.tex` (the `keep-tex: true` copy) may or may not have been updated
- Quarto's internal `index.tex` (what xelatex actually compiled) is separate and
  is not the same as `Vis-MLM.tex`
- The PDF may be from a previous successful build

This means that when debugging, you cannot assume that `Vis-MLM.tex` on
disk corresponds to the current source or the current freeze cache. Check
file timestamps. They may tell a different story than you expect — especially
on Windows, where NTFS timestamps sometimes lag or are preserved across
certain file operations.

## TeXStudio as a Fallback for LaTeX Errors

When Quarto's xelatex step fails, `Vis-MLM.tex` (from the last successful build)
can still be compiled manually in TeXStudio. This is valuable for:

- Iterating on LaTeX errors without re-running knitr and pandoc
- Getting a working PDF while the Quarto pipeline is being debugged

**Caveat:** `Vis-MLM.tex` may be from an older build and not reflect recent
source changes. The authorindex data is in `index.ain` (Quarto's name),
not `Vis-MLM.ain` (TeXStudio's expected name). Fix:

```bash
cp index.ain Vis-MLM.ain
```

Then compile `Vis-MLM.tex` in TeXStudio normally.

---

# Timeline

| Date | Event |
|------|-------|
| Pre-Apr 4 | HTML + PDF builds working; author index working |
| Apr 4 | Began fixing `_` in `func()` (index underscore problem) |
| Apr 4 | First `Extra }, or forgotten \endgroup` errors appear |
| Apr 4–10 | Multiple failed fix attempts: removed `\n` from `dataset()`, deleted freeze caches, moved `parse-latex` filter to HTML-only, upgraded to Quarto 1.9.36 then reverted to 1.8.25 as a diagnostic (ruled out Quarto as cause) |
| Apr 10 | Added `\def\citeproc#1#2{#2}` to `preamble.tex` (partial fix) |
| Apr 12 | Freeze cache regenerated (April 12 timestamps on `tex.json`) but `Vis-MLM.tex` still showing April 10 — stale keep-tex copy; Quarto compiles `index.tex` internally |
| Apr 13 | Root cause #1 identified: `\@citex` patch in `preamble.tex` causes `Extra }` when `\citeproc` is inside `\footnote{}` |
| Apr 13 | Root cause #2 identified: `parse-latex.lua` FORMAT guard (`FORMAT:match 'latex'`) was wrong — Quarto's PDF pandoc pass uses a non-"latex" FORMAT value; filter was running for PDF, expanding `\ixd{}` macros |
| Apr 13 | Fixes applied: disabled `\@citex` patch; removed `\n` from `pkg()` and `package()`; fixed parse-latex.lua guard to `not FORMAT:match 'html'` |
| Apr 13 | Confirmed: `Vis-MLM.tex` (April 10) compiles in TeXStudio; `cp index.ain Vis-MLM.ain` restores author index |

---

# Appendix: The Quarto–Pandoc–LaTeX Tool Chain for This Book

```
.qmd source
  ↓  knitr (R execution)
     · inline `r expr` → raw LaTeX strings via common.R functions
     · results cached in .quarto/_freeze/*/execute-results/tex.json
  ↓  pandoc (markdown → LaTeX)
     · expands @citations via built-in citeproc → \citeproc{key}{text}
     · wraps multi-line raw inline LaTeX with % at line breaks
     · applies Lua filters (parse-latex guard: FORMAT:match 'html' only)
     · embeds preamble.tex via include-in-header
  ↓  index.tex (Quarto's internal LaTeX file — named after entry point index.qmd)
     · Vis-MLM.tex is a keep-tex:true copy, written only on successful builds
  ↓  XeLaTeX (first pass)
     · \AtBeginDocument hooks fire (including old \@citex patch)
     · \citeproc inside \footnote{} triggers the bug here ← THE BUG
  ↓  makeindex → index.ind file (subject index)
  ↓  authorindex Perl script → index.ain file (author index)
     · NOTE: index.ain, not Vis-MLM.ain — must cp index.ain Vis-MLM.ain
       when compiling Vis-MLM.tex manually in TeXStudio
  ↓  XeLaTeX (second pass) → index.pdf (renamed Vis-MLM.pdf / index.pdf)
```

---

*Written in the immediate aftermath of the fix, while the memory was fresh
and the relief was palpable.*
